persona.name: Diego Martín
persona.education: B.Eng. Telecommunications, Universitat Politècnica de València. Self-taught full-stack web development.
long_term_goals: Run a profitable two-person web agency that hosts every client's site on a single shared cluster he controls
Keep client onboarding cheap enough that small projects remain commercially viable

occupation.title: Co-founder & Technical Lead
occupation.organization: Estudio Marina (boutique web agency, 2 people, ~35 active client sites)
occupation.description: Builds and operates web applications for a portfolio of small and medium clients (restaurants, regional NGOs, professional services). Owns hosting, deploys, security, and support. The other founder handles design and client relationships.

persona.communication_style: Warm, friendly, slightly informal. Switches into highly technical mode with peers. Translates technical risk into client-business language for non-technical stakeholders.

traits: Optimizes for repeatability — every client should look the same operationally
Treats per-client snowflake configuration as the worst possible outcome
Will spend a week on tooling to save a day per future client

big_five_traits.openness: High — happy to try new platforms when they reduce per-client toil
big_five_traits.conscientiousness: High — keeps a per-client deployment journal and runs monthly restore tests
big_five_traits.extraversion: Moderate-high — comfortable on client calls and at local meetups
big_five_traits.agreeableness: High — strong client-relationship instincts, but firm on technical hygiene
big_five_traits.neuroticism: Moderate — anxious about a single shared cluster taking down all clients at once

likes: Per-tenant isolation that does not require provisioning new servers
Subdomain-based routing baked into the runtime
Atomic deploys with instant rollback per client
dislikes: Shared databases where one client's bad query can impact another
Hosting platforms that price per project rather than per cluster

skills: Backup, restore, and incident response for shared infrastructure
beliefs: A boutique agency only survives if every client looks the same operationally
Tenant isolation must be enforced by the runtime, not by developer discipline
Blast radius from one client's failure should never reach another's data

projects.title: Estudio Marina Shared Hosting Cluster
projects.description: A single shared cluster hosting all client web apps. Currently a hand-rolled Docker Compose setup behind nginx with per-client SQLite databases on a single rented bare-metal box. Pain points: per-client isolation is by convention only, secrets management is ad hoc, rolling back a single client's deploy is awkward.
key_contributions: Designed the per-client SQLite isolation pattern that lets him host 35+ small apps on one box
Wrote the deployment script that templates an nginx vhost and a Docker Compose service per client

general: Refuses to onboard a client whose hosting needs cannot be templated
Tracks per-client uptime and incident count on a single dashboard
Tests restore procedures for every client database monthly

morning: Checks the multi-client status dashboard before opening client emails
workday: Builds new client features in three-hour focused blocks
Pairs with his co-founder on client deliverables and timelines
Runs weekly internal cleanup sweeps of unused per-client resources
evening: Catches up on web platform changelogs and self-hosting communities
Maintains his internal toolbox of agency scripts
weekend: Volunteers technical help for a local NGO that is also a client

relationships.name: Marina Costa
relationships.description: Co-founder of Estudio Marina; leads design and client relationships. Trusts Diego with all technical decisions and complains good-naturedly about his preference for boring technology.

relationships.name: Client portfolio
relationships.description: Around 35 active clients ranging from one-page restaurant sites to small regional NGO portals with logins and donation flows.

other_facts: current_stack: Single bare-metal Hetzner box running Docker Compose, nginx for TLS and routing, per-client SQLite databases on disk, restic backups to S3.
current_stack: Each client gets a Docker Compose service and an nginx vhost; secrets live in per-client .env files mounted into containers.
jobs_to_be_done: Host many isolated small client apps on one shared cluster behind subdomain routing.
jobs_to_be_done: Give each client its own KV+SQL+secrets without provisioning a new database server per client.
jobs_to_be_done: Roll back a single client's deploy without touching any other client.
pains: One client's runaway query or memory leak can degrade all other clients on the box.
pains: Per-client secrets and config drift across .env files he edits manually.
pains: Onboarding a new client still requires editing nginx vhosts and Compose files by hand.
adoption_yes_if: freq-cloud routes by Host header so each client gets a clean subdomain without nginx surgery.
adoption_yes_if: Per-tenant SQLite, KV, secrets, and quotas are isolated by the runtime, not by his discipline.
adoption_yes_if: He can roll back any single client to any prior bundle hash without touching others.
adoption_yes_if: Per-tenant resource quotas hard-cap blast radius from one client to another.
rejection_no_if: He would have to provision separate runtime instances per client to get isolation.
rejection_no_if: There is no way to set per-client storage and KV quotas at runtime.
rejection_no_if: A single client's failure can crash the runtime for all other clients.
anti_goals: He does not want to operate Kubernetes or a complex orchestrator just to host small client sites.
anti_goals: He does not need multi-region or autoscaling.
anti_goals: He is uninterested in elaborate observability stacks for sites that get a few hundred visits a day.
recognition_cues: Phrases like '35 client sites on one box', 'per-client subdomain', 'blast radius', 'one tenant cannot affect another', 'per-client database'.
recognition_cues: Asks specifically about per-tenant quotas and per-tenant secrets.
recognition_cues: Frames decisions in terms of per-client onboarding cost and operational uniformity.
